file_name: Díaz - 2020 - Deep artificial vision applied to the early identification of non-melanoma cancer and actinic kerato.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed a digital image processing system based on deep artificial vision for the analysis of non-melanoma cancer and actinic keratosis. It utilized mathematical modeling and simulation of algorithms, including image segmentation, binarization, and a pre-trained neural network (DnCNN) for noise reduction, to detect morphological and pigmentation irregularities in skin lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original results on the development of a digital image processing system based on deep artificial vision for analyzing non-melanoma cancer and actinic keratosis. It details a specific methodology involving mathematical modeling, algorithm simulation (including segmentation, binarization, and neural networks like DnCNN), and implementation in MATLAB to detect morphological and pigmentation irregularities, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Deep Artificial Vision Applied to the Early Identification of Non-Melanoma Cancer and Actinic Keratosis
brief_summary: This study developed a digital image processing system based on deep artificial vision to assist in the early identification of non-melanoma cancer and actinic keratosis. The methodology employed mathematical modeling and algorithm simulation, including segmentation, binarization, and a pre-trained DnCNN neural network for noise reduction, implemented in MATLAB. The system analyzes morphological and pigmentation characteristics of skin lesions, using the ABCDE model as a reference parameter for classification. Evaluation focused on the system's ability to isolate and detect irregularities in skin images to facilitate specialist diagnosis. The results demonstrated that the proposed algorithms effectively enhance image details and support the detection of anomalies associated with melanoma and other skin cancers. The study concludes that this tool can improve diagnostic accuracy and efficiency, with potential for future integration of advanced convolutional neural networks.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary focus of the study, specifically mentioning basal cell carcinoma and squamous cell carcinoma as types of non-melanoma cancer. The system was developed to analyze morphological and pigmentation characteristics of these lesions.
Studied alongside non-melanoma cancer as a condition appearing as abnormal pigmentation changes in the skin. The paper notes it often appears with non-melanoma cancer.

Not the primary target for the developed system's diagnosis, but used as the reference parameter (ABCDE model) for classification and analysis of skin lesions. The study uses melanoma characteristics to establish criteria for detecting irregularities in other lesions.
The physical entities analyzed in the study, which are subjected to image processing to determine if they are benign or malignant based on the ABCDE model parameters.

all_task_types.target: Diagnostic category of skin lesions (benign vs. malignant, non-melanoma cancer vs. actinic keratosis)
all_task_types.details: The paper describes a system for the 'early identification' and 'diagnosis' of non-melanoma cancer and actinic keratosis. It uses the ABCDE model (Asymmetry, Border, Color, Diameter, Evolution) as a reference parameter to classify lesions as benign or malignant. The system aims to facilitate the specialist's decision regarding the 'type of melanoma' (referring to skin cancer type in context) and subsequent treatment, which implies discrete class prediction rather than continuous value regression.

datasets_used.name: Custom Clinical Image Repository
datasets_used.description: A private repository of clinical skin images (moles or spots) captured by medical specialists, stored in a folder structure within the developed software system. Images were acquired via digital cameras, smartphones, or tablets in clinical or non-controlled environments.

use.testing: Used for evaluation of the image processing algorithms (segmentation, binarization, noise reduction) to detect morphological and pigmentation irregularities based on the ABCDE model.
use.other: The system was designed to process these images to assist specialists in diagnosis. The paper mentions 'preliminary tests' with standard neural networks but does not report a specific dataset split for training a new model; the DnCNN used was pre-trained.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. The study focuses on the development and algorithmic simulation of a digital image processing system for early identification of skin lesions, rather than statistical validation of diagnostic accuracy against a gold standard with inferential statistics.

Custom Clinical Image Repository.metric: Qualitative assessment of algorithm performance for morphological and pigmentation analysis

DnCNN.value: Effective in reducing noise to facilitate the detection of morphological and pigmentation irregularities

Canny.value: Identified as particularly interesting for detecting discontinuities (edges, lines, corners)

Sobel.value: Effective in eliminating noise and smoothing the image to reduce false edges

Roberts.value: Used for edge detection with specific masks

Prewitt.value: Used for edge detection with specific masks

Mahalanobis Distance.value: Provided better results than Euclidean distance for pixel distribution analysis in color or grayscale images

Euclidean Distance.value: Used to measure object diameter, resize images, and crop regions

Custom Clinical Image Repository.notes: The paper reports that the proposed algorithms effectively enhance image details and support the detection of anomalies. The Mahalanobis distance metric was found to be superior to Euclidean distance for analyzing pixel distributions. The system allows specialists to isolate and detect irregularities based on the ABCDE model parameters (Asymmetry, Border, Color, Diameter, Evolution). No quantitative metrics (e.g., accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) or statistical comparisons were reported.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained model used for noise reduction; specific pretraining dataset not reported.
architecture_used.input_size: Not reported (system accepts images from smartphones, tablets, or digital cameras; processed in grayscale or RGB channels).

architecture_details.purpose: Noise reduction (specifically Gaussian noise removal) to enhance image quality for subsequent morphological and pigmentation analysis.
architecture_details.limitations_noted: Only functions with Gaussian noise and limited standard deviation; works with single-channel 2D images; requires separate processing for multi-channel (3D/color) images.

training_details.details: The DnCNN model was pre-trained. The study did not report training the model on the specific clinical dataset. Future work mentions potential integration of deep convolutional neural networks and machine learning algorithms.

classification_or_output_head.details: The system does not use a neural network classifier head for final diagnosis. Classification is based on the ABCDE model (Asymmetry, Border, Color, Diameter, Evolution) parameters extracted via image processing algorithms (segmentation, binarization, edge detection, distance metrics) and interpreted by a specialist.

preprocessing_techniques: Conversion of RGB images to grayscale (single channel) to facilitate algorithm application
Image binarization (thresholding) to isolate regions of interest
Image complement calculation (inversion) to enhance low-light areas and details
Dehazing algorithm application for contrast enhancement and dynamic range maintenance

Morphological operations: dilation (adding pixels to boundaries), erosion (removing pixels from boundaries), opening (erosion followed by dilation), and closing (dilation followed by erosion) to clean contours and remove irrelevant objects
Edge detection using Canny, Sobel, Roberts, and Prewitt operators with specific masks and gradient calculations
Non-maximum suppression of gradients to obtain single-pixel width edges
Hysteresis thresholding to eliminate noise maxima and close open contours
Gaussian filtering for image smoothing to prevent edge over-detection
DnCNN (Denoising Convolutional Neural Network) for Gaussian noise reduction
Euclidean distance calculation for object diameter measurement, resizing, and cropping
Mahalanobis distance calculation for pixel distribution similarity analysis
conclusions: The developed digital image processing system based on deep artificial vision effectively isolates and detects morphological and pigmentation irregularities in skin lesions, facilitating specialist diagnosis.

The system utilizes the ABCDE model (Asymmetry, Border, Color, Diameter, Evolution) as a reference parameter for classifying lesions and guiding clinical decisions regarding non-melanoma cancer and actinic keratosis.
The Mahalanobis distance metric provided superior results compared to the Euclidean distance for analyzing pixel distribution similarity in color or grayscale images.
The Canny edge detection algorithm was identified as particularly effective for detecting discontinuities such as edges, lines, and corners.
The Sobel algorithm was found to be effective in eliminating noise and smoothing images to reduce false edges.
The DnCNN (Denoising Convolutional Neural Network) was effective in reducing Gaussian noise to facilitate the detection of morphological and pigmentation irregularities.
The system allows specialists to interpret and infer the type of anomaly found in the skin, improving the prospect of functional software for decision-making regarding treatment.

The study acknowledges that image capture variables, such as lighting conditions and human factors (e.g., specialist fatigue), influence the analysis, necessitating the use of standardized reference parameters within the software.
Future work suggests the integration of advanced convolutional neural networks and machine learning algorithms to further enhance the system's capabilities.
important_remarks: The study lacks quantitative performance metrics (e.g., accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) and statistical validation; results are based on qualitative assessment and algorithmic simulation rather than rigorous clinical evaluation.

The dataset is a private, custom repository of clinical images captured in non-controlled environments (via smartphones, tablets, or digital cameras), introducing significant bias regarding image quality, lighting, and capture variability.

The DnCNN model used for noise reduction was pre-trained and not trained on the study's specific clinical dataset; it is limited to Gaussian noise with a limited standard deviation and single-channel 2D images, requiring separate processing for multi-channel (color) images.

The system does not perform automated classification; it serves as a decision support tool that extracts features based on the ABCDE model for interpretation by a specialist, relying on the specialist's expertise to make the final diagnosis.

The study acknowledges that external variables such as lighting conditions, skin cleanliness, and human factors (e.g., specialist fatigue) can lead to false positives or erroneous analysis, highlighting the need for standardized reference parameters.

There is no external validation reported; the system was evaluated using preliminary tests and standard neural networks within the context of the developed software, limiting the generalizability of the findings.

The paper notes a lack of existing computerized systems for early detection of non-melanoma cancer and actinic keratosis in the reviewed literature, positioning this work as a novel attempt to address this gap, though without comparative benchmarks against existing gold-standard methods.
